title: REST API Basics: Resources, HTTP Verbs, and Status Codes
excerpt: A practical beginner-friendly guide to REST APIs: what resources are, how GET/POST/PUT/DELETE work, and which HTTP status codes matter most.

value.title: What a REST API actually is

body.value:
A REST API is a way for systems to communicate over HTTP using clearly defined endpoints.

In practice, it lets one application ask another application for data or request that something be created, updated, or removed.

The most important idea is this: the API exposes resources, and clients interact with those resources using standard HTTP methods.

value.title: Key idea
value.text: Think of a REST API as a contract for working with business resources through HTTP.

body.value:
A resource is usually a business object such as a user, order, invoice, or product.

Endpoints should be named around those resources, not around actions.

  • /api/users
  • /api/orders
  • /api/products

This is more scalable and easier to understand than action-based naming.

• GET - read data
  • POST - create new data
  • PUT - replace existing data
  • PATCH - partially update existing data
  • DELETE - remove data

Even when the back-end is complex, the external API should keep this behavior predictable.

value.caption: Basic REST interaction
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Client] -->|GET /api/orders| B[REST API]
    B --> C[(Database)]
    C --> B
    B -->|200 OK + JSON| A

body.value:
Status codes are part of the API contract. They tell the client what happened.

  • 200 OK - request succeeded
  • 201 Created - new resource created
  • 204 No Content - success with no response body
  • 400 Bad Request - invalid input
  • 401 Unauthorized - authentication required
  • 403 Forbidden - authenticated but not allowed
  • 404 Not Found - resource does not exist
  • 500 Internal Server Error - unexpected server failure

value.title: Practical tip
value.text: Use status codes consistently. Clients should not need to guess whether an operation succeeded.

body.value:
Most REST APIs exchange data as JSON because it is lightweight, readable, and supported almost everywhere.

A typical response might return a collection, a single resource, metadata, or validation errors.

Good APIs use plural nouns, stable URLs, and clear resource hierarchy.

  • /api/customers
  • /api/customers/15/orders

Avoid names like /getOrders or /createUser because HTTP verbs already describe the action.

value.title: Simple controller example in ASP.NET Core

value.caption: Minimal REST-style controller example

body.value:
A good REST API is built around resources, uses HTTP methods consistently, returns meaningful status codes, and keeps naming predictable.

Once these basics are clear, topics like authentication, versioning, pagination, and API security become much easier to understand.

title: Designing RESTful Web APIs — A Practical Guide from Fundamentals to Production
excerpt: Learn how to design RESTful APIs that scale, evolve, and remain maintainable. Covers HTTP fundamentals, resources, verbs, paging, caching, versioning, and security.

value.title: Why API Design Comes Before Implementation

body.value:
One of the biggest mistakes developers make is starting to build an API before designing it. Unlike internal code, APIs become contracts. Once clients depend on them, changing them becomes difficult or even impossible.

A well-designed API is long-lasting, predictable, and easy to evolve. This is why design must come first — not code.

REST APIs are built on top of HTTP, so understanding HTTP is essential. Every request consists of:

  • Verb (what to do)
  • Headers (metadata)
  • Body (data)

Every response contains:

  • Status code
  • Headers
  • Body

value.caption: Basic HTTP request-response flow
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Client -->|Request: GET /customers| Server
Server -->|Response: 200 OK + JSON| Client

body.value:
REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style based on:

  • Client-server separation
  • Stateless communication
  • Cacheable responses
  • Uniform interface

In practice, REST is about designing intuitive, predictable APIs — not strictly following theory.

In REST, everything is a resource. Resources are represented via URIs:

  • /customers → collection
  • /customers/1 → single resource

Query strings should be used only for optional behavior like filtering or paging.

REST uses HTTP verbs to describe actions:

  • GET → retrieve
  • POST → create
  • PUT → update (full)
  • PATCH → update (partial)
  • DELETE → remove

value.caption: REST operations on resources
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
Collection[/customers/] -->|POST| NewCustomer
Collection -->|GET| ListCustomers
Item[/customers/{id}/] -->|GET| GetCustomer
Item -->|PUT| UpdateCustomer
Item -->|DELETE| DeleteCustomer

body.value:
Idempotency means repeating the same request produces the same result.

  • GET, PUT, DELETE → idempotent
  • POST → NOT idempotent

This is critical for reliability in distributed systems.

Responses should be:

  • Consistent
  • Technology-agnostic
  • Client-friendly

Use JSON conventions like camelCase and avoid leaking database structure.

REST APIs should express relationships via URIs:

  • /customers/1/invoices
  • /projects/22/tickets

This makes APIs intuitive and self-explanatory.

value.caption: Resource relationships
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
Customer --> Invoices
Customer --> Projects
Project --> Tickets

body.value:
Never return unbounded collections. Always support paging.

Use query strings like ?page=1&pageSize=25.

Errors should be meaningful and structured.

Use status codes + error body:

Use HTTP caching to reduce load.

ETags allow clients to ask: “Has this changed?”

value.caption: ETag caching flow
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
Client->>Server: GET /resource
Server-->>Client: 200 OK + ETag
Client->>Server: GET + If-None-Match
Server-->>Client: 304 Not Modified

body.value:
APIs must evolve without breaking clients.

  • Query string: ?v=1
  • Header: X-Version
  • Content type: application/vnd.api.v1+json

Consistency is more important than the method you choose.

Security must be part of design, not an afterthought.

  • Use HTTPS
  • Implement authentication
  • Apply authorization rules

value.caption: Token authentication flow
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
Client->>Server: Login (credentials)
Server-->>Client: JWT Token
Client->>Server: Request + Token
Server-->>Client: Response

body.value:
• Design APIs before implementing
  • Think in resources, not database tables
  • Use HTTP correctly (verbs, status codes)
  • Plan for paging, caching, and versioning
  • Secure your API from day one

Great APIs are not accidental — they are designed.

title: ASP.NET Core — Big Picture Explained
excerpt: Understand what ASP.NET Core is, how it works, and how to build modern web applications using MVC, Blazor, APIs, and more.

body.value:
ASP.NET Core is a modern, cross-platform web application framework built by Microsoft. It allows developers to build both frontend and backend applications using C# and .NET.

It is not just for websites — it is a complete platform for building full web systems including APIs, real-time apps, and modern client-side applications.

value.title: Key idea
value.text: ASP.NET Core is a unified platform for building web applications, APIs, and real-time systems using .NET.

body.value:
A website typically serves static content such as HTML and CSS files.

A web application introduces dynamic behavior — for example fetching data from a database and rendering it on the page.

  • Static site → fixed HTML
  • Web app → dynamic data + logic

value.caption: Static vs dynamic web application
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Browser] --> B[Static HTML]
    A --> C[Web Application]
    C --> D[(Database)]
    D --> C
    C --> A

body.value:
Modern applications separate responsibilities into two layers:

  • Frontend → UI, user interaction
  • Backend → business logic, data access

This separation improves maintainability, scalability, and reusability.

value.caption: Frontend and backend separation
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Browser UI] --> B[Backend API]
    B --> C[(Database)]
    C --> B
    B --> A

value.title: Why separation matters
value.text: A backend API can be reused by web, mobile, and desktop applications.

value.title: Ways to build UI in ASP.NET Core

body.value:
ASP.NET Core provides multiple ways to build frontend applications:

  • MVC → structured pattern (Model-View-Controller)
  • Razor Pages → simpler page-based approach
  • Blazor → modern component-based UI

In server-rendered applications, the server generates HTML and sends it to the browser for each request.

Every interaction (click, form submit) triggers a new request.

value.caption: Server-rendered flow
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
    Browser->>Server: Request page
    Server->>Database: Fetch data
    Database-->>Server: Data
    Server-->>Browser: HTML response

body.value:
Client-rendered applications shift processing to the browser.

The server sends application logic once, and the browser handles UI updates.

value.caption: Client-rendered SPA flow
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
    Browser->>Server: Initial request
    Server-->>Browser: App + runtime
    Browser->>API: Fetch data
    API-->>Browser: JSON
    Browser: Render UI

value.title: Key difference
value.text: Server-rendered = HTML per request. Client-rendered = UI handled in browser.

body.value:
A Web API is a backend application that exposes data via HTTP.

  • GET → retrieve data
  • POST → create data
  • PUT → update data

value.caption: REST API interaction
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Client] -->|GET /products| B[API]
    B --> C[(Database)]
    C --> B
    B --> A

value.caption: Example API endpoint

body.value:
ASP.NET Core processes requests using a pipeline of middleware.

Each middleware can inspect or modify the request and response.

value.caption: Request pipeline
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Request] --> B[Routing]
    B --> C[Authorization]
    C --> D[Controller]
    D --> E[Response]

body.value:
Dependency Injection (DI) allows you to register services and request them anywhere in your application.

This makes code modular, testable, and maintainable.

value.title: Why DI matters
value.text: You don’t create dependencies manually — the framework injects them for you.

body.value:
• Multiple frontend approaches (MVC, Razor Pages, Blazor)
  • Powerful backend APIs (REST, gRPC)
  • Built-in dependency injection
  • Flexible middleware pipeline
  • Cross-platform performance

title: ASP.NET Core Web API Fundamentals: From Zero to First API
excerpt: Learn the fundamentals of building APIs with ASP.NET Core, including architecture, middleware, routing, and real-world API design concepts.

body.value:
ASP.NET Core is a cross-platform, high-performance, open-source framework for building modern web applications and APIs. It allows developers to create scalable, cloud-ready applications that run on Windows, Linux, and macOS.

It is commonly used for building REST APIs that communicate with databases, external services, and frontend applications.

At a high level, an ASP.NET Core API processes incoming HTTP requests through a pipeline of components called middleware. Each component can inspect, modify, or stop the request before passing it further.

value.diagram:
graph TD
Client --> Request
Request --> Middleware1
Middleware1 --> Middleware2
Middleware2 --> Controller
Controller --> Response
Response --> Client

body.value: This pipeline is one of the most important concepts in ASP.NET Core. It defines how your application responds to every request.

Middleware are small components executed in sequence. Each middleware can:

  • Handle the request
  • Modify the request or response
  • Stop the pipeline early

The order of middleware is critical. For example, authentication must run before protected endpoints.

value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
Client->>Middleware1: HTTP Request
Middleware1->>Middleware2: Forward
Middleware2->>Controller: Execute action
Controller-->>Middleware2: Result
Middleware2-->>Middleware1: Response
Middleware1-->>Client: Final Response

body.value: The application starts in Program.cs, which configures services and builds the application pipeline.

This file defines:

  • Services (Dependency Injection)
  • Middleware pipeline
  • Application startup behavior

value.title: Understanding the MVC Pattern in APIs

body.value:
ASP.NET Core APIs are typically built using the MVC pattern (Model-View-Controller).

In APIs, the concept of "View" is different — it usually represents JSON output instead of HTML.

value.diagram:
graph TD
Client --> Controller
Controller --> Model
Model --> Controller
Controller --> JSON_Response
JSON_Response --> Client

body.value:
Controller: Handles requests
Model: Represents data
View: JSON response
Routing maps HTTP requests to controller actions. In ASP.NET Core, this is typically done using attribute routing.
This means a GET request to /api/cities will trigger the GetCities method.

APIs rely on HTTP methods to define operations:

  • GET – retrieve data
  • POST – create data
  • PUT – update data
  • DELETE – remove data
Status codes tell the client whether a request succeeded or failed. They are essential for proper API behavior.

value.diagram:
graph TD
Request --> API
API -->|200 OK| Success
API -->|404 Not Found| MissingResource
API -->|400 Bad Request| ClientError
API -->|500 Internal Server Error| ServerError

body.value: Returning incorrect status codes can break client applications and make debugging difficult.
Tools like Postman allow you to send HTTP requests and inspect responses. This is essential when testing APIs beyond simple browser GET requests.

• ASP.NET Core is a powerful framework for building APIs
  • The request pipeline is built using middleware
  • MVC is the standard pattern for API design
  • Routing connects URLs to controller actions
  • Status codes are critical for communication

title: Debugging and Error Handling in ASP.NET Core Web APIs
excerpt: Learn how to properly debug and handle errors in ASP.NET Core Web APIs using Swagger, Postman, filters, and global exception handling strategies.

value.title: Why debugging and error handling matter

body.value:
In modern web APIs, error handling is not just a technical detail — it directly impacts developer experience. APIs must not expose internal exceptions, but instead return structured, meaningful responses with proper status codes.

A well-designed API should be easy to debug, easy to test, and predictable in how it communicates failures.

value.title: Key principle
value.text: Never expose raw exceptions to clients. Always return structured, meaningful error responses.

value.caption: Request processing and error flow
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
 Client --> Controller
 Controller --> Service
 Service --> Database
 Database --> Service
 Service --> Controller
 Controller --> Response

 Controller -->|Exception| ErrorHandler
 ErrorHandler --> Response

body.value: Errors can occur at any stage — controller, service, or database. Without proper handling, these errors propagate and expose internal details. A proper error handling strategy intercepts these failures and transforms them into consistent responses.

value.title: Documenting and testing APIs with Swagger

body.value: Swagger provides automatic API documentation and a UI for testing endpoints. It removes the need to manually remember endpoints and payload structures.
Swagger can be extended using metadata and XML comments, making it a powerful documentation tool for teams.

value.title: Testing APIs using Swagger UI and Postman

body.value: Swagger UI allows quick testing of endpoints directly from the browser, while Postman provides advanced testing capabilities such as collections, automation, and scripting.

value.caption: Debugging workflow
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
 Developer->>SwaggerUI: Test endpoint
 Developer->>Postman: Advanced testing
 Postman->>API: Send request
 API-->>Postman: Response
 Postman->>Developer: Debug result

body.value: ASP.NET Core supports multiple environments such as Development and Production. Each environment can have different configurations, especially for error handling and security.
This endpoint ensures all unhandled exceptions are converted into a structured ProblemDetails response.

value.title: Using filters for fine-grained error handling

body.value: Filters allow you to intercept requests at different stages of execution. Exception filters are particularly useful for handling specific exceptions.

value.caption: ASP.NET Core filter pipeline
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
 Authorization --> Resource --> Action --> Exception --> Result

value.caption: Custom exception filter

body.value: ASP.NET Core provides the ProblemDetails standard for error responses. It ensures consistent structure across all API errors.

value.caption: Global exception middleware
Custom exception interface

body.value: Using a shared interface allows different parts of your system to throw consistent, structured exceptions.
Logging ensures that even if clients receive sanitized messages, developers still have full visibility into failures.

To build robust ASP.NET Core APIs:

  • Use Swagger for documentation and testing
  • Use Postman for advanced debugging
  • Handle errors globally
  • Standardize responses using ProblemDetails
  • Use custom exceptions for domain logic
  • Log everything important

Combining these techniques results in APIs that are easier to maintain, debug, and integrate with.

title: Authentication and Authorization in ASP.NET Core Web API — Complete Guide
excerpt: Learn how to secure ASP.NET Core Web APIs using API keys, cookies, tokens, OAuth2, OpenID Connect, and authorization policies with practical architecture diagrams and examples.

body.value:
APIs are the heart of modern applications. They expose data and business logic, making them critical assets that must be protected. Securing an API means answering two fundamental questions:

  • Who are you? (Authentication)
  • What are you allowed to do? (Authorization)

This guide walks through all major approaches available in ASP.NET Core, from simple API keys to modern OAuth2 and OpenID Connect.

value.title: Key concept
value.text: Authentication proves identity. Authorization defines permissions.

value.caption: Frontend calling API
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Browser] --> B[Frontend App]
    B --> C[ASP.NET Core API]
    C --> D[(Database)]
    D --> C
    C --> B
    B --> A

body.value: A typical setup consists of a frontend application (MVC, Blazor, SPA) that communicates with an ASP.NET Core Web API. The API handles data and business logic.
The simplest way to protect an API is using an API key. The client sends a secret value in an HTTP header, and the API validates it.

value.caption: Checking API key in middleware

value.title: Important limitation
value.text: API keys do not identify users and are hard to rotate or revoke. Use them only for simple machine-to-machine scenarios.

body.value:
Cookies are used when users log into a web application. After authentication, an identity cookie is issued and automatically sent with each request.

This works well when frontend and backend share the same domain.

value.caption: Cookie authentication flow
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
    Browser->>Frontend: Request protected page
    Frontend->>User: Show login
    User->>Frontend: Credentials
    Frontend->>Browser: Set identity cookie
    Browser->>Frontend: Request with cookie
    Frontend->>API: Forward request
    API->>Frontend: Response

body.value: Tokens replace cookies when applications are not browser-based. A token is issued after login and sent in the Authorization header.

value.caption: OAuth2 architecture
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Client App] --> B[Identity Provider]
    B -->|Access Token| A
    A --> C[API]
    C --> A

body.value: OAuth2 introduces a central Identity Provider (IdP) that issues tokens. This enables secure, scalable architectures with multiple applications.
This flow is used when no user is involved. A client authenticates using a Client ID and Secret to obtain an access token.

value.caption: Client Credentials Flow
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
    Client->>Identity Provider: ClientId + Secret
    Identity Provider->>Client: Access Token
    Client->>API: Request + Token
    API->>Client: Response

body.value: When users are involved, the Authorization Code Flow is used. The frontend redirects the user to the Identity Provider for login, and receives tokens after successful authentication.

value.caption: Authorization Code Flow
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
    Browser->>Frontend: Request
    Frontend->>Identity Provider: Redirect to login
    Identity Provider->>Browser: Login page
    Browser->>Identity Provider: Credentials
    Identity Provider->>Frontend: Authorization Code
    Frontend->>Identity Provider: Exchange code
    Identity Provider->>Frontend: Access + Identity Token
    Frontend->>API: Request with token

body.value: JWT tokens contain three parts: header, payload, and signature. They are Base64 encoded and can be inspected.

value.title: Security insight
value.text: JWTs are not encrypted by default — always use HTTPS to protect them.

body.value: Once authenticated, APIs use claims inside the token to make authorization decisions. ASP.NET Core provides policies for centralized authorization logic.

In real-world systems, multiple applications and APIs interact. Authorization should be based on scopes rather than user roles alone, because not all flows include users.

  • Use scopes for API-level access
  • Use roles when users are present
  • Use policies to centralize logic

value.caption: Authorization API pattern
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Client] --> B[Backend API]
    B --> C[Authorization API]
    C --> B
    B --> A

body.value: For complex systems, a dedicated Authorization API can provide user permissions dynamically instead of embedding everything in tokens.

• API Keys → simple, limited security
  • Cookies → browser-based apps
  • Tokens → flexible, scalable
  • OAuth2 + OpenID Connect → enterprise-grade solution

For modern distributed systems, OAuth2 with JWT tokens and centralized identity providers is the recommended approach.

title: Logging and Monitoring in ASP.NET Core: From Basics to Production-Ready Observability
excerpt: Learn how logging, monitoring, and traceability work in ASP.NET Core. Understand log levels, filters, structured logging, and how to build production-ready observability into your applications.

body.value:
Good logging and monitoring can dramatically reduce the time needed to diagnose and fix issues. In many cases, the difference between a quick fix and hours of debugging comes down to how well your application logs information.

Logging is not just about writing messages — it is about making your system observable, understandable, and supportable in real-world conditions.

value.title: Key Insight
value.text: Well-designed logging can turn production debugging from guesswork into a deterministic process.

value.title: How Logging Works in ASP.NET Core

body.value:
ASP.NET Core provides built-in logging through the Microsoft.Extensions.Logging framework. This system is based on three main concepts:

  • Loggers (ILogger)
  • Providers (destinations)
  • Configuration (levels and filters)

value.caption: Logging architecture in ASP.NET Core
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Application Code] --> B[ILogger]
    B --> C[Logging Framework]
    C --> D[Console]
    C --> E[Debug Output]
    C --> F[File / External Systems]

body.value:
When you create a new ASP.NET Core application, logging is already configured by default. The framework automatically logs to:

  • Console
  • Debug output
  • Event source

This is initialized through WebApplication.CreateBuilder(), which wires up logging providers and default filters.

value.caption: Injecting ILogger into a controller

value.title: Important
value.text: Use message templates instead of string interpolation — this enables structured logging and better querying.

body.value:
Instead of interpolating strings, ASP.NET Core logging uses message templates:

"Getting products for {category}"

This allows logging systems to store structured data (e.g., category separately), enabling powerful filtering and querying later.

ASP.NET Core defines six log levels:

  • Trace – very detailed diagnostics
  • Debug – debugging information
  • Information – normal application flow
  • Warning – unexpected but non-breaking issues
  • Error – failures in specific operations
  • Critical – system-wide failures

value.caption: Log level hierarchy
value.diagram:
flowchart TB
    Trace --> Debug --> Information --> Warning --> Error --> Critical

body.value:
Filters determine which logs are written. They are based on:

  • Log level
  • Category (usually class name)
  • Provider

Example configuration:

Each log entry has a category, usually derived from the class name:

ILogger<ProductController> → Namespace.ProductController

You can also define custom categories using ILoggerFactory.

Monitoring ensures your application is running correctly. ASP.NET Core supports health checks to evaluate system status.

These checks can validate:

  • Database connectivity
  • External APIs
  • System resources

value.caption: Health check flow
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Monitoring System] --> B[Health Endpoint]
    B --> C[Database]
    B --> D[External API]
    B --> E[Application]
    C --> B
    D --> B
    E --> B
    B --> A

body.value:
Proper exception handling is critical for both security and supportability.

  • Log full exception details internally
  • Do NOT expose stack traces to users
  • Use global exception handling
  • Provide user-friendly error messages

value.title: Security Warning
value.text: Never expose exception details in production — logs are for developers, not users.

body.value: In modern applications, especially microservices, a single request can flow through multiple systems. Traceability allows you to follow that journey.

value.caption: Distributed request tracing
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
    participant UI
    participant API
    participant ServiceA
    participant ServiceB

    UI->>API: Request
    API->>ServiceA: Call
    ServiceA->>ServiceB: Call
    ServiceB-->>ServiceA: Response
    ServiceA-->>API: Response
    API-->>UI: Response

body.value: Using tools like OpenTelemetry, you can correlate logs, traces, and metrics across services.

• Logging is essential for support and debugging
  • Use structured logging with message templates
  • Control noise with levels and filters
  • Monitor application health proactively
  • Handle exceptions globally and securely
  • Use tracing for distributed systems

title: Logging and Monitoring in ASP.NET Core: From Basics to Production-Ready Observability

Monitoring ensures your application is running correctly. ASP.NET Core supports health checks to evaluate system status.

  • External APIs
  • System resources

value.caption: Health check flow
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Monitoring System] --> B[Health Endpoint]
    B --> C[Database]
    B --> D[External API]
    B --> E[Application]
    C --> B
    D --> B
    E --> B
    B --> A

body.value:
Proper exception handling is critical for both security and supportability.

  • Log full exception details internally
  • Do NOT expose stack traces to users
  • Use global exception handling
  • Provide user-friendly error messages

value.title: Security Warning
value.text: Never expose exception details in production — logs are for developers, not users.

body.value: In modern applications, especially microservices, a single request can flow through multiple systems. Traceability allows you to follow that journey.

value.caption: Distributed request tracing
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
    participant UI
    participant API
    participant ServiceA
    participant ServiceB

    UI->>API: Request
    API->>ServiceA: Call
    ServiceA->>ServiceB: Call
    ServiceB-->>ServiceA: Response
    ServiceA-->>API: Response

body.value: Using tools like OpenTelemetry, you can correlate logs, traces, and metrics across services.

• Logging is essential for support and debugging
  • Use structured logging with message templates
  • Control noise with levels and filters
  • Monitor application health proactively
  • Handle exceptions globally and securely
  • Use tracing for distributed systems

title: Documenting ASP.NET Core APIs with OpenAPI (Swagger) — Complete Guide
excerpt: Learn how to document ASP.NET Core Web APIs using OpenAPI (Swagger), including setup, customization, versioning, authentication, and advanced scenarios like vendor-specific media types.

body.value:
API documentation is not optional — it is a critical part of building scalable systems. Without proper documentation, teams waste time asking the same questions, duplicate functionality, or misuse endpoints.

A well-documented API ensures:

  • Faster onboarding for developers
  • Reduced support overhead
  • Better adoption across teams
  • Clear contract between backend and consumers

OpenAPI is a standard specification for describing REST APIs in a machine-readable format (JSON or YAML). It defines endpoints, parameters, responses, and schemas.

Swagger is a set of tools built around this specification.

value.caption: OpenAPI Ecosystem
value.diagram:
graph TD
API[ASP.NET Core API] --> Generator[Swashbuckle]
Generator --> Spec[OpenAPI JSON]
Spec --> UI[Swagger UI]
Spec --> Client[Client SDK Generator]
Spec --> Tests[Automated Tests]

value.caption: Basic Swagger configuration

value.text: Swagger consists of two separate parts: the OpenAPI specification (JSON) and the UI that renders it. They are independent.

body.value:
The OpenAPI specification is generated automatically by inspecting your controllers using ApiExplorer. It analyzes:

  • Routes and HTTP methods
  • Parameters
  • Return types
  • Attributes and metadata

value.caption: Swagger Generation Flow
value.diagram:
graph LR
Controllers --> ApiExplorer
ApiExplorer --> Swashbuckle
Swashbuckle --> OpenAPI
OpenAPI --> SwaggerUI

value.caption: XML comments example

body.value: These comments are included in Swagger when XML documentation is enabled and configured in the project.

value.caption: Validation attributes

body.value: Swagger automatically reflects validation rules like required fields and max length in the documentation UI.

value.text: Always explicitly define response codes. Default Swagger generation is often incomplete.

value.caption: Content Negotiation
value.diagram:
graph TD
Client -->|Accept: application/json| API
Client -->|Accept: application/xml| API
API --> JSON
API --> XML

body.value: Standard media types like application/json only describe format, not structure. Vendor-specific media types allow you to define multiple representations.

value.caption: Multiple Representations
value.diagram:
graph TD
Request -->|v1| BookV1
Request -->|v2| BookV2

value.caption: API Versioning
value.diagram:
graph TD
Client -->|v1| APIv1
Client -->|v2| APIv2

body.value: Authentication must be part of your API documentation so consumers know how to interact with protected endpoints.

value.text: Swagger UI can be fully customized using CSS, JavaScript, or even a custom HTML index page.

body.value:
• OpenAPI is a standard for describing APIs
  • Swagger tools generate UI and clients from the spec
  • Swashbuckle automates spec generation in ASP.NET Core
  • Always explicitly define responses and media types
  • Support versioning and authentication in documentation
  • Advanced APIs require customization beyond defaults
